retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Triangle -- Structure and Function -- Anal Canal
retrieved_docs.content: The anal canal is the hindmost part of the gastrointestinal system. [1] There is both an anatomic and surgical definition of the anal canal. The anatomic anal canal spans from the anal verge to the pectinate (dentate) line. The surgical anal canal is longer and spans from the anal verge to the anorectal line. [2] The anal verge is the junction of anoderm with perianal skin.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Ischioanal Fossa -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The perianal space is located at the level of the anal orifice. Continuous with the ischioanal space laterally and superiorly, it continues between the external and internal sphincter. The ischiorectal space (or ischioanal space) extends from the levator ani muscle to the perineum lateral to the anal canal. The intersphincteric space is located between the internal and external sphincters; it continues inferiorly into the perianal space and superiorly into the rectum wall. The supra levator space (or pelvic-rectal space) is limited superiorly by the peritoneum, laterally by the pelvic wall, medially by the rectal wall, and inferiorly by the levator ani muscle. The deep post-anal space is found at the front of the apex of the coccyx. It is limited superiorly by the plate of the levator ani muscle and inferiorly by the anococcygeal raphe. It is the posterior part of the ischioanal fossa where the 2 spaces meet.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adductor Canal (Subsartorial Canal, Hunter Canal) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Proximal border - The AC begins at the femoral triangle's apex, where the medial borders of the sartorius and adductor longus cross. Some sources cite the femoral triangle apex as the adductor longus' lateral border. However, recent studies maintain that the apex is the medial border of the adductor longus muscle. Distal border - The AC ends at the adductor hiatus, the largest of 5 fibrous openings within the adductor magnus. The superficial femoral artery becomes the popliteal artery as it passes distally through the adductor hiatus. Anterolateral border - The vastus medialis muscle lies in the AC's anterolateral margin. Posterolateral border - The AC is bounded by the adductor longus and magnus muscles posterolaterally. Medial border - The vastoadductor membrane (VAM) occupies the AC's medial edge. The VAM is also sometimes referred to as the AC's “roof.”. The sartorius muscle is superficial to the VAM.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemicolectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Right Colon
retrieved_docs.content: The right colon starts in a blind-ending pouch called the cecum, located in the right iliac fossa of the abdomen. The ascending colon is covered by a layer of peritoneum covering its anterior and lateral surfaces. The hepatic flexure lies intraperitoneal adjacent to the liver in the right hypochondrium. The paracolic gutter lies lateral to the ascending and descending colon and acts as a collecting point or conduit for free fluid in the peritoneal cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: Appendectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Typically, the appendix is located in the right lower quadrant of the abdomen. However, it may also be found in the left upper quadrant, left anterior para-midline, and lower midline. It may also be positioned in several other orientations, such as the pelvic, where it points towards the psoas muscle and hangs over the pelvic brim; sub-cecal, where it is in the iliac fossa and inferior to the cecum; retro-ileal, where it lies posterior to the ileum; and pre-ileal, where it is anterior to the ileum. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Adductor Canal (Subsartorial Canal, Hunter Canal) -- Structure and Function -- Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Published literature regarding the location of the AC's roof and medial border is inconsistent. Some sources name the AC's medial border as the sartorius muscle, with the canal located deep to this muscle and the muscle's span determining the AC's limits. However, the VAM lies deep to the sartorius muscle. The true AC is roofed and bordered medially by the VAM.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Large Intestine -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The cecum, the proximal blind pouch of the ascending colon, is located at the ileocecal junction in the right iliac fossa. The terminal ileum opens into the cecum through the medial wall. The ileocecal valve regulates this passage. Although the cecum lacks a mesentery, it remains highly mobile within the right iliac fossa. The appendix, a thin cylindrical structure measuring approximately 6 to 10 cm, is attached to the cecum at its posteromedial wall, about 1 to 2 cm below the ileocecal junction. The tip of the appendix often extends into the peritoneal cavity, most commonly resting in a retrocecal position. A short, triangular mesentery, the mesoappendix, provides vascular supply to the appendix.

retrieved_docs.title: Appendectomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The vermiform appendix is a small, tubular, blind-ended hollow outpouching attached to the posteromedial wall of the cecum, typically 1.7 cm below the ileocecal valve. The base of the appendix arises from the convergence of the taeniae coli, making this point of convergence a useful surgical landmark. The human appendix is typically 8 to 10 cm long in adults and is considered a vestigial organ. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Foramen of Winslow (Omental Foramen, Epiploic Foramen) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The foramen of Winslow is an oddity for the anatomist. The foramen of Winslow is defined superiorly by the caudate lobe of the liver and dorsally by the inferior vena cava. The inferior portion is defined by the duodenum, with the hepatoduodenal ligament serving as the ventral border. The foramen of Winslow is the only natural communication between the greater peritoneal cavity and the lesser sac. Also known as the epiploic foramen or the omental foramen, this small window was described by Jacob Winslow in his 1732 publication, Exposition anatomique de la structure du corps human (see Image. Foramen of Winslow).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Appendix -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The official Terminologia Anatomica name of the appendix is "Appendix Vermiformis". The appendix, a true diverticulum arising from the posteromedial cecal border, is located in close proximity to the ileocecal valve. The base of the appendix can be reliably located near the convergence of the taeniae coli at the tip of the cecum. The term vermiform is Latin for worm-like [1] and ascribes to its long, tubular architecture. In contrast to an acquired diverticulum, it is a true diverticulum of the colon and contains all of the colonic layers: mucosa, submucosa, longitudinal and circular muscularis propria and serosa. The histological distinction between the colon and the appendix depends upon the presence of B and T lymphoid cells in the appendicular mucosa and submucosa. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Large Intestine -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The appendix and the transverse and sigmoid colonic segments are intraperitoneal structures. However, the cecum is also intraperitoneal, as it is covered by the peritoneum on all sides and lacks a mesentery. The right and left colon, the rectum, and the anal canal are retroperitoneal structures.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Large Intestine -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The cecum is continuous with the 2nd part of the large intestine, the ascending (right) colon. This part of the large bowel runs superiorly along the right side of the abdomen, extending from the right iliac fossa to the right lobe of the liver. At this point, the right colon makes a left turn at the right colic (hepatic) flexure to continue as the transverse colon. The ascending colon is a retroperitoneal organ, with the right paracolic gutter—a deep vertical groove—separating its lateral side from the adjacent abdominal wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Gallbladder -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The triangle of Calot is of surgical importance. It has the following boundaries: 1) cystic duct on the right, 2) common hepatic duct on the left, and 3) the undersurface of the liver superiorly. The original definition of this triangle from 1891 listed the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and the cystic artery as boundaries. This has since been modified to allow for better identification of landmarks for locating the cystic artery. Of surgical importance is that within the modern definition of the triangle of Calot, it runs the cystic artery and lymph node. Sometimes, the gallbladder neck is connected to the first part of the duodenum with loose fibrous bands known as the peritoneal cholecystoduodenal fold.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Large Intestine -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The large intestine is the portion of the digestive tract where water is absorbed from indigestible material (see Image . Large Intestine). This section of the gut includes the cecum, appendix, entire colon, rectum, and anal canal. The large intestine begins at the terminal ileum with the cecum. Unlike the small intestine, the large bowel is shorter but has a much larger lumen. This segment is further distinguished from the small intestine by the presence of omental appendices, haustra, and teniae coli. [1] [2] [3] The omental appendices are small pouches of peritoneum filled with fat. The haustra are small pouches or sacculations that give the large intestine its segmented appearance. The teniae coli are 3 longitudinal bands of smooth muscle on the outer wall of the colon.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Triangle -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The obturator internus muscle is the lateral border of the ischiorectal fossae.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 185

Overall, the knowledge emphasizes precise identification of anatomical sites such as the vaginal wall and awareness of complications like fistula to guide effective surgical management of pelvic floor disorders and related diverticula.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: The peritoneum serves as the anatomical finding site for فتق البطن (abdominal hernia) and is also the location implicated in اضطراب صِفَاقِيّ (serosal disorder), indicating that peritoneal involvement is central to both the presentation of abdominal hernias and the development of serosal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 81
retrieved_docs.content: surgical approach. The shared clinical goal across all listed procedures is the restoration of normal anatomy and function by closing abnormal fistulous tracts, thereby guiding clinicians in selecting the optimal operative technique based on fistula location, morphology, and required reconstruction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: anchored by the pathology النَّاسُور. The hierarchical organization and the repeated use of the abdominal, transanal, vaginal, and combined approaches enable clinicians to select the most appropriate operative technique for the specific anatomical site while addressing the common fistulous morphology, thereby supporting informed decision‑making and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: abdominal hernia (فتق البطن), and biliary disorders (اِضطِراب صِفَاقِيّ). Clinical findings such as an abdominal mass (كتلة في البَطْن) or acute abdomen (بَطْنٌ حادّ) are evaluated with the same imaging and procedural approaches. Understanding the interconnected anatomy of the abdomen and liver, the spectrum of hepatic diseases, their risk factors, and the potential toxicities of substances used in abdominal interventions is essential for safe procedural planning, accurate diagnosis, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1850
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal pregnancy (حمل منتبذ) is clinically associated with laparotomy (شق البطن), because surgical entry into the abdominal cavity is frequently required for the diagnosis, evaluation, and definitive management of this rare ectopic gestation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 210
retrieved_docs.content: modalities, procedural interventions, and disease processes are anatomically and clinically related, providing a comprehensive framework for the evaluation and management of abdominal, hepatic, and biliary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2022
retrieved_docs.content: Exploratory laparotomy and ثقب معوي (intestinal perforation) are frequently observed together in clinical practice. An exploratory laparotomy is often performed when a ثقب معوي is suspected or identified, as the surgical approach allows direct visualization and management of the perforated bowel. Recognizing the co‑occurrence of these conditions is important for timely diagnosis and appropriate operative intervention, which can reduce morbidity and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 211
retrieved_docs.content: are recurring morphological themes. The treatment of bladder cyst (علاج القيلة المثانية) and rectal cyst (علاج قيلة مستقيمية) is directly linked to fistula morphology, underscoring fistulas as a common structural concern in both conditions. Accurate differentiation of combined procedures from standalone cystourethroscopy and precise coding of anatomical sites are essential for clear clinical documentation and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: with fistula formation when treated; treatment of bladder diverticulum (علاج القيلة المثانية) and treatment of rectal diverticulum (علاج قيلة مستقيمية) are both noted to have potential fistulous complications. All of these conditions share the common pathophysiological mechanism of organ descent or herniation into the vaginal canal, and their management frequently involves direct intervention on the vaginal walls, correction of obstructive changes, and prevention or treatment of fistulous sequelae.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 796
retrieved_docs.content: diverticulum treatment and rectal prolapse treatment involve fistulous pathology. Overall, the relationships delineate how anatomical sites (anterior and posterior vaginal walls), specific prolapse subtypes, associated obstruction and fistulae, and hierarchical classifications inform the diagnosis and coordinated surgical treatment of pelvic organ prolapse and related fistulous conditions in female patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of gastrointestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). As a subset of this wider gastrointestinal spectrum, anal and rectal conditions affect the distal portion of the digestive tract and share many pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic considerations, and therapeutic strategies with other gastrointestinal disorders. Recognizing that anal and rectal conditions have a broader relationship with gastrointestinal disorder encourages clinicians to adopt a comprehensive, system‑based approach to evaluation and management, ensuring that patient care addresses both localized anorectal pathology and its context within the entire gastrointestinal system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
retrieved_docs.content: The closure of rectovaginal fistula by transanal approach, the repair of rectovaginal fistula, the repair of sigmoidovaginal fistula, the repair of vaginocutaneous fistula, and the repair of vaginoperineal fistula all share the same direct morphology, which is described by the term “النَّاسُور.” This common morphological characteristic indicates that, despite involving different anatomical sites, these fistula‑related procedures address a similar structural feature. Recognizing that “النَّاسُور” underlies the pathology of each of these fistulas can aid clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical techniques and in anticipating comparable intra‑operative considerations across these varied but morphologically related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 111
retrieved_docs.content: and frequently co‑occur with peritonitis (الْتِهابُ الصِّفاقِ). Fallopian tube surgeries (العمليات الجراحية لقناة فالوب) are considered a specific subset of abdominal surgeries. Overall, these relationships illustrate how abdominal destructive procedures, renal endoscopic interventions, and peritoneal surgeries are interconnected through shared anatomical sites (abdomen, kidneys, ureter, peritoneum), procedural classifications, and associated clinical findings, emphasizing the integrated nature of urologic and abdominal surgical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic ultrasound of gastrointestinal tract is a diagnostic imaging procedure that is performed directly on the gastrointestinal system, enabling visualization of the organs and structures within the gastrointestinal tract for clinical assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2906
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic approach for bladder diverticulum (علاج القيلة المثانية) is directly linked to the morphological presence of a fistula (فَتْق), while the therapeutic approach for rectal diverticulum (علاج قيلة مستقيمية) is associated with the pathological condition of a fistula (فَتْق). Both treatment contexts therefore involve considerations of fistula formation as a key clinical feature.
